From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Class of synthetic fiber related to nylon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Para_Aramid_Yarns_Body_Armor.jpg/250px-Para_Aramid_Yarns_Body_Armor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Para_Aramid_Yarns_Body_Armor.jpg)

Spools of aramid yarn destined for body armor

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Glass_aramid_hybrid_area.jpg/250px-Glass_aramid_hybrid_area.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glass_aramid_hybrid_area.jpg)

Fiberglass–aramid hybrid cloth

**Aramid,**  or **aromatic polyamide** fibers are a class of strong, heat-resistant, [synthetic fibers](/wiki/Synthetic_fiber "Synthetic fiber"), commonly used in aerospace and military applications - e.g., ballistic-rated [body armor](/wiki/Bulletproof_vest "Bulletproof vest") [fabric](/wiki/Cloth "Cloth") and ballistic composites, [marine cordage](/wiki/Marine_cordage "Marine cordage") and [hull](/wiki/Marine_hull "Marine hull") reinforcement - as a substitute for [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos"),[[1]](#cite_note-Hillermeier-1) and in lightweight consumer items, such as [phone cases](/wiki/Phone_case "Phone case") and [tennis rackets](/wiki/Tennis_racket "Tennis racket").

Individual amide molecules forming the aramid chain [polymerise](/wiki/Polymerization "Polymerization") in the direction of the fiber axis, lending greater structural integrity to the resulting fiber. This is due to the higher proportion of [chemical bonds](/wiki/Chemical_bonds "Chemical bonds") which contribute to the physical strength and thermal resistance (melting point >500 °C (932 °F)) versus other synthetic fibres, such as [nylon](/wiki/Nylon "Nylon").

Notable [brands](/wiki/Brand_name "Brand name") of aramid fiber include [Kevlar](/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar"), [Nomex](/wiki/Nomex "Nomex"), and [Twaron](/wiki/Twaron "Twaron").

## Terminology and chemical structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology and chemical structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Kevlar_chemical_structure_H-bonds.svg/500px-Kevlar_chemical_structure_H-bonds.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kevlar_chemical_structure_H-bonds.svg)

Structure of [Twaron](/wiki/Twaron "Twaron") and [Kevlar](/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar"). The [aromatic rings](/wiki/Aromaticity "Aromaticity") appear as hexagons. The rings are attached alternately to either two NH groups or two CO groups.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The attachment points on each ring are diametrically opposite each other, a characteristic of the structure called [para](/wiki/Para-_(chemistry) "Para- (chemistry)")-aramid.

The term *aramid* is shortened from *[aromatic](/wiki/Aromatic "Aromatic") [polyamide](/wiki/Polyamide "Polyamide")*. It was introduced in 1972,[[3]](#cite_note-3) accepted in 1974 by the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") of the USA as the name of a generic category of fiber distinct from [nylon](/wiki/Nylon "Nylon"),[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FTC-5) and adopted by the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") in 1977.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*Aromatic* in the longer name refers to the presence of [aromatic rings](/wiki/Aromaticity "Aromaticity") of six carbon atoms. In aramids these rings are connected via [amide](/wiki/Amide "Amide") linkages each comprising a CO group attached to an NH group.

In order to meet the FTC definition of an aramid,[[5]](#cite_note-FTC-5) at least 85% of these linkages must be attached to two aromatic rings.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Below 85%, the material is instead classed as nylon.[[5]](#cite_note-FTC-5)

### Para-aramids and meta-aramids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Para-aramids and meta-aramids")]

Aramids are divided into two main types according to [where the linkages attach](/wiki/Arene_substitution_pattern#Ortho,_meta,_and_para_substitution "Arene substitution pattern") to the rings. Numbering the carbon atoms sequentially around a ring, **para-aramids** have the linkages attached at positions 1 and 4, while **meta-aramids** have them at positions 1 and 3.[[7]](#cite_note-7) That is, the attachment points are diametrically opposite each other in para-aramids, and two atoms apart in meta-aramids. The illustration thus shows a para-aramid.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Staal_van_aramidevezel_van_het_merk_Kevlar.jpg/250px-Staal_van_aramidevezel_van_het_merk_Kevlar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Staal_van_aramidevezel_van_het_merk_Kevlar.jpg)

Kevlar brand aramid rope

Aromatic polyamides were first introduced in [commercial](/wiki/Commerce "Commerce") applications in the early 1960s, with a [meta](/wiki/Meta-_(chemistry) "Meta- (chemistry)")-aramid fiber produced by [DuPont](/wiki/DuPont_(1802%E2%80%932017) "DuPont (1802–2017)") as HT-1 and then under the trade name [Nomex](/wiki/Nomex "Nomex").[[8]](#cite_note-Kent-8) This fiber, which handles similarly to normal textile apparel fibers, is characterized by its excellent resistance to heat, as it neither melts nor ignites in normal levels of [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"). It is used extensively in the production of protective apparel, air filtration, thermal and electrical insulation, and as a substitute for [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos").

Meta-aramids are also produced in the Netherlands and Japan by [Teijin Aramid](/wiki/Teijin_Aramid "Teijin Aramid") under the trade name Teijinconex,[[8]](#cite_note-Kent-8) and by [Toray](/wiki/Toray "Toray") under the trade name Arawin, in [China](/wiki/China "China") by Yantai Tayho under the trade name New Star and by SRO Group under the trade name X-Fiper, and a variant of meta-aramid in [France](/wiki/France "France") by Kermel under the trade name Kermel**.**

Based on earlier research by [Monsanto Company](/wiki/Monsanto "Monsanto") and [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer"), [para](/wiki/Para-_(chemistry) "Para- (chemistry)")-aramid fiber with much higher [tenacity](/wiki/Tensile_strength "Tensile strength") and [elastic modulus](/wiki/Elastic_modulus "Elastic modulus") was also developed in the 1960s and 1970s by DuPont and [AkzoNobel](/wiki/AkzoNobel "AkzoNobel"), both profiting from their knowledge of [rayon](/wiki/Rayon "Rayon"), [polyester](/wiki/Polyester "Polyester") and nylon processing. In 1973, DuPont was the first company to introduce a para-aramid fiber, calling it Kevlar; this remains one of the best-known[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] para-aramids or aramids.

In 1978, Akzo introduced a similar fiber with roughly the same chemical structure calling it [Twaron](/wiki/Twaron "Twaron"). Due to earlier patents on the production process, Akzo and DuPont engaged in a patent dispute in the 1980s. Twaron subsequently came under the ownership of the Teijin Aramid Company. In 2011, Yantai Tayho introduced similar fiber which is called Taparan in [China](/wiki/China "China") (see [Production](#Production)).

Para-aramids are used in many high-tech applications, such as aerospace and military applications, for "bullet-proof" [body armor](/wiki/Bulletproof_vest "Bulletproof vest") [fabric](/wiki/Cloth "Cloth").

Both meta-aramid and para-aramid fiber can be used to make aramid paper. Aramid paper is used as electrical insulation materials and construction materials to make honeycomb core. Dupont made aramid paper during the 1960s, calling it Nomex paper. Yantai Metastar Special Paper introduced an aramid paper in 2007, which is called metastar paper. Both Dupont and Yantai Metastar make meta-aramid and para-aramid paper.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Health")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Textielmuseum-cabinet-17.jpg/250px-Textielmuseum-cabinet-17.jpg)](/wiki/File:Textielmuseum-cabinet-17.jpg)

Display of aramid and carbon fiber products at the Textielmuseum in [Tilburg](/wiki/Tilburg "Tilburg"). Clockwise from top right: combined aramid–carbon fiber braided textile, various carbon-fiber-reinforced composites, carbon yarn and woven textile, aramid [Twaron](/wiki/Twaron "Twaron") glove, braided glass fiber cable with aramid core, aramid yarn.

During the 1990s, an *[in vitro](/wiki/In_vitro "In vitro")* test of aramid fibers showed they exhibited "many of the same effects on [epithelial cells](/wiki/Epithelial_cell "Epithelial cell") as did [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos"), including increased [radiolabeled](/wiki/Radiolabel "Radiolabel") [nucleotide](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") incorporation into DNA and induction of ODC ([ornithine decarboxylase](/wiki/Ornithine_decarboxylase "Ornithine decarboxylase")) enzyme activity", raising the possibility of [carcinogenic](/wiki/Carcinogenic "Carcinogenic") implications.[[9]](#cite_note-toxicology-effects-9) However, in 2009, it was shown that inhaled aramid fibrils are shortened and quickly cleared from the body and pose little risk.[[10]](#cite_note-toxicology-review-10) A declaration of interest correction was later provided by the author of the study stating that "This review was commissioned and funded by DuPont and Teijin Aramid, but the author alone was responsible for the content and writing of the paper."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Aramid-Prozess.svg/250px-Aramid-Prozess.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Aramid-Prozess.svg)

Aramid process diagram

World capacity of para-aramid production was estimated at 41,000 t (40,000 long tons; 45,000 short tons) per year in 2002 and increases each year by 5–10%.[[12]](#cite_note-capacity-12) In 2007 this means a total production capacity of around 55,000 tonnes per year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Polymer preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Polymer preparation")]

Aramids are generally prepared by the reaction between an [amine](/wiki/Amine "Amine") group and a [carboxylic acid](/wiki/Carboxylic_acid "Carboxylic acid") [halide](/wiki/Halide "Halide") group. Simple AB [homopolymers](/wiki/Homopolymer "Homopolymer") have the connectivity −(NH−C6H4−CO)*n*−.

Well-known aramid polymers such as Kevlar, Twaron, Nomex, New Star, and Teijinconex) are prepared from diamine and diacid (or equivalent) precursors. These polymers can be further classified according to the linkages on the aromatic subunits. Nomex, Teijinconex, and New Star contain predominantly the meta-linkage. They are called poly-metaphenylene isophthalamides (MPIAs). By contrast, Kevlar and Twaron both feature para-linkages. They are called *p*-phenylene terephthalamides (PPTAs). PPTA is a product of [*p*-phenylene diamine](/wiki/P-Phenylenediamine "P-Phenylenediamine") (PPD) and [terephthaloyl dichloride (TDC or TCl)](/wiki/Terephthaloyl_chloride "Terephthaloyl chloride").

Production of PPTA relies on a [cosolvent](/wiki/Cosolvent "Cosolvent") with an [ionic](/wiki/Ion "Ion") component ([calcium chloride](/wiki/Calcium_chloride "Calcium chloride"), CaCl2) to occupy the [hydrogen bonds](/wiki/Hydrogen_bond "Hydrogen bond") of the amide groups, and an organic component ([*N*-methyl pyrrolidone](/wiki/N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone "N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone"), NMP) to dissolve the [aromatic](/wiki/Aromatic "Aromatic") [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer"). This process was invented by Leo Vollbracht at Akzo. Apart from the [carcinogenic](/wiki/Carcinogenic "Carcinogenic") [HMPT](/wiki/Hexamethylphosphoramide "Hexamethylphosphoramide"), still no practical alternative of dissolving the polymer is known. The use of the NMP/CaCl2 system led to an extended patent dispute between Akzo and DuPont.

### Spinning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Spinning")]

After production of the polymer, the aramid fiber is produced by [spinning](/wiki/Spinneret_(polymers) "Spinneret (polymers)") the dissolved polymer to a solid fiber from a liquid chemical blend. Polymer solvent for spinning PPTA is generally 100% [anhydrous](/wiki/Anhydrous "Anhydrous") [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid") (H2SO4).

### Appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Appearances")]

* [Fiber](/wiki/Fiber "Fiber")
* Chopped fiber
* [Powder](/wiki/Powder_(substance) "Powder (substance)")
* [Pulp](/wiki/Pulping "Pulping")

## Other types of aramids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other types of aramids")]

Besides meta-aramids like Nomex, other variations belong to the aramid fiber range. These are mainly of the **copolyamide** type, best known under the brand name [Technora](/wiki/Technora "Technora"), as developed by Teijin and introduced in 1976. The manufacturing process of Technora reacts PPD and 3,4'-diaminodiphenylether (3,4'-ODA) with [terephthaloyl chloride (TCl)](/wiki/Terephthaloyl_chloride "Terephthaloyl chloride").[[13]](#cite_note-13)
This relatively simple process uses only one amide solvent, and therefore spinning can be done directly after the polymer production.

## Aramid fiber characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Aramid fiber characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Ankerwinde.jpg/250px-Ankerwinde.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ankerwinde.jpg)

Aramid anchor rope used on board the MV Bornholm in the port of [Delfzijl](/wiki/Delfzijl "Delfzijl"), June 2006

Aramids share a high degree of orientation with other fibers such as [ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene](/wiki/Ultra-high-molecular-weight_polyethylene "Ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene"), a characteristic that dominates their properties.

### General

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: General")]

* good resistance to [abrasion](/wiki/Wear#Abrasive_wear "Wear")
* good resistance to organic solvents
* nonconductive [*[ambiguous](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
* very high [melting point](/wiki/Melting_point "Melting point") (>500 °C (932 °F))
* low flammability
* good fabric integrity at elevated [temperatures](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature")
* sensitive to acids and salts [*[how?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
* sensitive to [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet") [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") [*[how?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
* prone to electrostatic charge build-up unless finished[[14]](#cite_note-textiles-14)

### Para-aramids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Para-aramids")]

* para-aramid fibers, such as Kevlar and Twaron, provide outstanding strength-to-weight properties
* high [chord modulus](/wiki/Chord_modulus "Chord modulus")
* high [tenacity](/wiki/Tensile_strength "Tensile strength")
* low [creep](/wiki/Creep_(deformation) "Creep (deformation)")
* low elongation at break (~3.5%)
* difficult to dye – usually [solution-dyed](/wiki/Wet_processing_engineering#Solution_dyeing "Wet processing engineering")[[14]](#cite_note-textiles-14)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aramid&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Uses")]

* flame-resistant clothing
* heat-protective clothing and [helmets](/wiki/Helmet "Helmet")
* [body armor](/wiki/Bulletproof_vest "Bulletproof vest"),[[15]](#cite_note-bulletproof-15) competing with [polyethylene](/wiki/Polyethylene "Polyethylene")-based fiber products such as [Dyneema](/wiki/Dyneema "Dyneema") and [Spectra](/wiki/Ultra_high_molecular_weight_polyethylene "Ultra high molecular weight polyethylene")
* [composite materials](/wiki/Composite_material "Composite material")
* [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") replacement (e.g. [brake linings](/wiki/Brake_lining "Brake lining"))
* hot air [filtration](/wiki/Filtration "Filtration") [fabrics](/wiki/Textile "Textile")
* [tires](/wiki/Tire "Tire"), newly as Sulfron (sulfur-modified [Twaron](/wiki/Twaron "Twaron"))
* mechanical [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") goods reinforcement
* [ropes](/wiki/Rope "Rope") and [cables](/wiki/Wire_rope "Wire rope")[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [V-belts](/wiki/Belt_(mechanical) "Belt (mechanical)") (automotive, machinery, equipment, and more)
* wicks for [fire dancing](/wiki/Fire_dancing "Fire dancing")
* [optical fiber cable](/wiki/Optical_fiber_cable "Optical fiber cable") systems
* [sail cloth](/wiki/Sailcloth "Sailcloth") (not necessarily racing [boat](/wiki/Boat "Boat") sails)
* [sporting](/wiki/Sport "Sport") goods
* [drumheads](/wiki/Drum "Drum")
* wind instrument [reeds](/wiki/Reed_(instrument) "Reed (instrument)"), such as the Fibracell brand
* [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") diaphragms
* boathull material
* [fiber-reinforced concrete](/wiki/Fiber-reinforced_concrete "Fiber-reinforced concrete")
* [reinforced thermoplastic pipes](/wiki/Reinforced_thermoplastic_pipe "Reinforced thermoplastic pipe")
* [tennis strings](/wiki/Strings_(tennis) "Strings (tennis)"), e.g. by Ashaway and Prince tennis companies
* [hockey sticks](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick") (normally in composition with such materials as wood and carbon)
* [snowboards](/wiki/Snowboard "Snowboard")
* [jet engine](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine") enclosures
* [fishing reel](/wiki/Fishing_reel "Fishing reel") drag systems
* [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete") reinforcement
* [Prusiks](/wiki/Prusik "Prusik") for rock climbers (which slide along the main rope and can otherwise melt due to friction).
* [Snowboards](/wiki/Snowboards "Snowboards") core reinforcement
* [Mobile Phone](/wiki/Mobile_Phone "Mobile Phone") case
